judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 more clearly hits the RESULTS_DB collision risk (b/d... specifically d) with explicit corruption language and mitigation, but fails the F-11 early-concurrency staging requirement by deferring all tests to a later wave; Report 1 nails the staging requirement but soft-pedals the RESULTS_DB risk as "harmless now" — overall the two reports split the must-hits, with Report 2 slightly stronger on risk articulation and Report 1 slightly stronger on staging design.

report_1.evidence: Stream A — pipeline/registry.py (one engineer): F-05 (DEFAULT_RUBRIC_VERSION → v3), F-09 (DEFAULT_DATASET → fixtures/eval_set.jsonl), and F-02 (aurora-1 endpoint) once unblocked. All three are edits to the same ~11-line file; giving them to one person avoids three-way merge churn.

report_2.evidence: Engineer A — registry.py: F-02, F-05, F-09. Three tiny, independent config edits in one file; kept with one owner so they don't collide.

report_1.evidence: Then all four streams run simultaneously (Wave 1) — they touch disjoint files so there is no ordering constraint between streams. ... Stream C — pipeline/rubric.py (one engineer): F-01 ... and F-11 (edge-case parsing test)

report_2.evidence: Wave 2 (tests, sequenced after their code lands): F-11 (rubric edge-case parsing) depends on F-01, and F-12 (pagination boundary) depends on F-04 ... Run them after Wave 1 folds in so the new tests pass

report_1.evidence: F-06 — a judgment call, not a mechanical fix. "Too lenient / tighten them" gives no target numbers; changing thresholds shifts all downstream scores and historical comparability. It needs the reviewer/product owner to specify the new (low, high) values (and ideally a regression fixture) before an engineer touches it.

report_2.evidence: F-06 is a behavior/policy change, not a mechanical fix. ... a user-facing scoring-policy decision, not a settled bug. Per project documentation, I'd escalate it as a flagged batch (what should the new thresholds be? whose sign-off? does it invalidate previously stored scores?) and not let an engineer pick numbers unilaterally.

report_1.evidence: RESULTS_DB awareness — tests/conftest.py reads RESULTS_DB (default /var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db); harmless now but engineers should know it can be overridden via env.

report_2.evidence: Shared test resource collision. tests/conftest.py:4 defaults RESULTS_DB to a single shared path /var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db. Parallel pytest runs across engineers would read/write the same DB and cross-contaminate ... A distinct RESULTS_DB value exported per engineer (override of the conftest.py:4 default) so parallel test runs use isolated result databases instead of the shared /var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db.
